title: Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock II (unlimited transactions).
description: Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock II (unlimited transactions).

examples.explanation:
Day 0 (price=7, not holding): Option1: buy at 7 → Day1 (holding, buyPrice=7); Option2: skip → Day1 (not holding)
Day1 (price=1, not holding from skip path): Option1: buy at 1 → Day2 (holding, buyPrice=1); Option2: skip → Day2 (not holding)
Day1 (price=1, holding from buy at 7 path): Option1: sell at 1 → profit=-6, Day2 (not holding); Option2: skip → Day2 (holding, buyPrice=7)
... Continuing exploration...
Best path found: Buy at day1 (price=1), sell at day2 (price=5) → profit=4; then buy at day3 (price=3), sell at day4 (price=6) → profit=3; total=7
All other paths yield ≤7 profit.

Day0 (price=1, not holding): buy at 1 → Day1 (holding, buyPrice=1); skip → Day1 (not holding)
Exploring buy path: Day1 (price=2, holding): sell at 2 → profit=1, Day2 (not holding); skip → Day2 (holding, buyPrice=1)
Sell path continues: Day2 (price=3, not holding): buy at 3 → Day3 (holding); skip → Day3 (not holding)
... After full exploration, best path: Buy at day0 (1), sell at day4 (5) → profit=4 OR multiple small transactions: buy1-sell2 (profit1), buy2-sell3 (profit1), buy3-sell4 (profit1), buy4-sell5 (profit1) total=4
Maximum profit=4

approaches.name: Brute Force O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible buy-sell sequences by considering each day as a buy, sell, or skip action, leading to exponential possibilities. Use this only for understanding the problem space, as it's impractical for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursive Exploration of All Buy-Sell Sequences
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all possible buy-sell sequences with unlimited transactions.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the 'Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II' problem using unlimited transactions. The intuition is to explore every possible sequence of buy and sell decisions across the price array, considering that we can buy and sell multiple times. At each day, we have three choices: if we don't hold a stock, we can either buy (start a transaction) or skip; if we hold a stock, we can either sell (complete a transaction) or skip. This creates a binary decision tree with exponential possibilities. The algorithm uses recursion with backtracking to explore all paths: we maintain a current index in the price array and a boolean flag indicating whether we currently hold a stock. At each recursive call, we consider the appropriate actions based on our holding status. If we hold, we can either sell (add profit = current price - buy price) and move to next day without holding, or skip (continue holding). If we don't hold, we can either buy (mark current price as buy price) and move to next day with holding, or skip. We recursively explore both branches and return the maximum profit. The base case is when we reach the end of the array - if we're holding a stock, we must sell it (or consider it unsold with zero additional profit). The time complexity is O(2^n) because at each day we typically have 2 choices (except some constraints), creating an exponential decision tree. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. This approach connects to understanding the problem space by showing all possible transaction sequences, though it's impractical for n > 20-25. Common mistakes include not handling the 'must sell if holding at end' constraint, double-counting profits, or having incorrect base cases. The real pattern is depth-first search with state tracking.

examples.trace:
Day 0 (price=7, not holding): Option1: buy at 7 → Day1 (holding, buyPrice=7); Option2: skip → Day1 (not holding)
Day1 (price=1, not holding from skip path): Option1: buy at 1 → Day2 (holding, buyPrice=1); Option2: skip → Day2 (not holding)
Day1 (price=1, holding from buy at 7 path): Option1: sell at 1 → profit=-6, Day2 (not holding); Option2: skip → Day2 (holding, buyPrice=7)
... Continuing exploration...
Best path found: Buy at day1 (price=1), sell at day2 (price=5) → profit=4; then buy at day3 (price=3), sell at day4 (price=6) → profit=3; total=7
All other paths yield ≤7 profit.

Day0 (price=1, not holding): buy at 1 → Day1 (holding, buyPrice=1); skip → Day1 (not holding)
Exploring buy path: Day1 (price=2, holding): sell at 2 → profit=1, Day2 (not holding); skip → Day2 (holding, buyPrice=1)
Sell path continues: Day2 (price=3, not holding): buy at 3 → Day3 (holding); skip → Day3 (not holding)
... After full exploration, best path: Buy at day0 (1), sell at day4 (5) → profit=4 OR multiple small transactions: buy1-sell2 (profit1), buy2-sell3 (profit1), buy3-sell4 (profit1), buy4-sell5 (profit1) total=4
Maximum profit=4

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Decision Function with State Tracking
ladders.desc: The recursive function that decides buy/sell/skip at each day based on current holding status.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that implements the decision-making logic for the brute force approach. The intuition is that at each step (day) in the price array, we need to make optimal decisions based on whether we currently own a stock (holding) or not. The function takes three parameters: current index i, boolean holding flag, and the buyPrice if holding. It returns the maximum profit achievable from position i to the end. The algorithm: if i == n (end of array), return 0 (or sell if holding for 0 additional profit). If holding: we can either sell at current price (profit = prices[i] - buyPrice) and recurse from i+1 with not holding, OR skip (continue holding) and recurse from i+1 with holding. Return max of these two options. If not holding: we can either buy at current price (set buyPrice = prices[i]) and recurse from i+1 with holding, OR skip and recurse from i+1 with not holding. Return max of these. This creates a complete exploration of all possibilities. Time complexity for this function on a subarray from i to end is O(2^(n-i)) in worst case. Space complexity is O(n-i) for recursion depth. This connects to L0 as the main recursive engine - L0 simply calls this function with i=0, holding=false. Common mistakes: forgetting to pass buyPrice when holding, incorrect base case handling, or not considering that you can't buy if already holding. The pattern is a recursive DFS with state parameters.

examples.trace:
i=0, holding=false, prices[0]=5
Option1: buy at 5 → recurse(i=1, holding=true, buyPrice=5)
  i=1, holding=true, prices[1]=7
  Option1a: sell at 7 → profit=7-5=2, recurse(i=2, holding=false) → base case returns 0, total=2
  Option1b: skip → recurse(i=2, holding=true) → base case returns 0 (can't sell at end), total=0
  max(2,0)=2
Option2: skip at 5 → recurse(i=1, holding=false)
  i=1, holding=false, prices[1]=7
  Option2a: buy at 7 → recurse(i=2, holding=true) → base case returns 0, total=0
  Option2b: skip → recurse(i=2, holding=false) → base case returns 0, total=0
  max(0,0)=0
max(2,0)=2

i=0, holding=false, prices[0]=3
Option1: buy at 3 → recurse(i=1, holding=true, buyPrice=3)
  i=1, holding=true, prices[1]=2
  Option1a: sell at 2 → profit=2-3=-1, recurse(i=2, holding=false)→0, total=-1
  Option1b: skip → recurse(i=2, holding=true)→0, total=0
  max(-1,0)=0
Option2: skip at 3 → recurse(i=1, holding=false)
  i=1, holding=false, prices[1]=2
  Option2a: buy at 2 → recurse(i=2, holding=true)→0, total=0
  Option2b: skip → recurse(i=2, holding=false)→0, total=0
  max(0,0)=0
max(0,0)=0

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Space Exploration with Buy/Hold/Sell Decisions
ladders.desc: The fundamental decision tree structure exploring all possible sequences of buy, hold, and sell actions.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logical structure of exploring all possible transaction sequences through state transitions. The intuition is modeling the problem as a state machine with two states: 'not holding' (can buy or skip) and 'holding' (can sell or skip). Each day represents a state transition opportunity. From 'not holding', we transition to 'holding' by buying (recording buy price), or stay in 'not holding' by skipping. From 'holding', we transition to 'not holding' by selling (recording profit), or stay in 'holding' by skipping. This creates a binary tree of possibilities where each path represents a complete transaction sequence. The algorithm explores this tree using recursion: at each node (day, state), we generate child nodes for each valid transition, propagate state information (holding flag, buy price if applicable), and continue to next day. When we reach the end of days, we sum profits along the path. We need to explore all root-to-leaf paths and find the maximum profit sum. Time complexity arises from the branching factor: typically 2 choices at each step, leading to O(2^n) paths. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This connects to L1 as the conceptual framework that L1 implements recursively. Common mistakes include allowing invalid transitions (buying while holding, selling while not holding), not tracking buy prices correctly, or missing the constraint that you must sell if holding at end. The pattern is exhaustive search over a state transition graph.

examples.trace:
Day0 (price=2, state=not holding):
Path1: buy → state becomes holding (buyPrice=2) → Day1 (price=3, state=holding)
  Path1a: sell → profit=3-2=1, state=not holding → END profit=1
  Path1b: skip → state=holding → END profit=0 (can't sell)
Path2: skip → state=not holding → Day1 (price=3, state=not holding)
  Path2a: buy → state=holding (buyPrice=3) → END profit=0 (can't sell)
  Path2b: skip → state=not holding → END profit=0
All profits: [1,0,0,0], maximum=1

Day0 (3, not holding): buy or skip
Buy path: holding(buy3) → Day1 (2, holding): sell or skip
  Sell: profit=-1, not holding → Day2 (4, not holding): buy or skip
    Buy: holding(buy4) → END profit=-1+0=-1
    Skip: not holding → END profit=-1+0=-1
  Skip: holding(buy3) → Day2 (4, holding): sell or skip
    Sell: profit=1, not holding → END profit=0+1=1
    Skip: holding → END profit=0
Skip path: not holding → Day1 (2, not holding): buy or skip
  Buy: holding(buy2) → Day2 (4, holding): sell or skip
    Sell: profit=2, not holding → END profit=2
    Skip: holding → END profit=0
  Skip: not holding → Day2 (4, not holding): buy or skip
    Buy: holding(buy4) → END profit=0
    Skip: not holding → END profit=0
Maximum profit=2 (from skip-buy-sell path)

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal with Profit Accumulation
ladders.desc: Implementing recursive depth-first search that accumulates profits along paths.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the recursive tree traversal mechanism that accumulates profits. The intuition is that we need to traverse all possible paths in a decision tree, keeping a running total of profits, and return the maximum. Each path represents a sequence of decisions (buy/sell/skip). When we make a sell decision, we add (current price - buy price) to the profit total for that path. The algorithm uses depth-first search: starting from root (day 0, not holding, profit=0), we recursively explore children. Base case: when we reach beyond last day (index >= n), return current accumulated profit. Recursive case: at current day i with current profit, if holding: explore sell child (profit += prices[i] - buyPrice, not holding, i+1) and skip child (profit unchanged, holding, i+1). If not holding: explore buy child (profit unchanged, holding with buyPrice=prices[i], i+1) and skip child (profit unchanged, not holding, i+1). Return maximum of child explorations. Time complexity for traversing from a node is exponential in remaining days. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L2 as the implementation mechanism for exploring the state space - L2 describes the state transitions, L3 implements the traversal. Common mistakes: not accumulating profits correctly (adding when should not, or vice versa), not passing profit as parameter, or having incorrect base case. The pattern is DFS with path-specific accumulation.

examples.trace:
Call1: i=0, holding=false, profit=0
  Explore buy: profit stays 0, holding=true, buyPrice=2, i=1
    Call2: i=1, holding=true, profit=0
      Explore sell: profit=0+(3-2)=1, holding=false, i=2
        Call3: i=2 (base case) return profit=1
      Explore skip: profit=0, holding=true, i=2
        Call4: i=2 (base case) return profit=0
      max(1,0)=1
  Explore skip: profit=0, holding=false, i=1
    Call5: i=1, holding=false, profit=0
      Explore buy: profit=0, holding=true, buyPrice=3, i=2
        Call6: i=2 (base case) return profit=0
      Explore skip: profit=0, holding=false, i=2
        Call7: i=2 (base case) return profit=0
      max(0,0)=0
  max(1,0)=1

Call1: i=0, holding=false, profit=0
  Explore buy: profit=0, holding=true, buyPrice=5, i=1
    Call2: i=1, holding=true, profit=0
      Explore sell: profit=0+(4-5)=-1, holding=false, i=2
        Call3: i=2 return -1
      Explore skip: profit=0, holding=true, i=2
        Call4: i=2 return 0
      max(-1,0)=0
  Explore skip: profit=0, holding=false, i=1
    Call5: i=1, holding=false, profit=0
      Explore buy: profit=0, holding=true, buyPrice=4, i=2
        Call6: i=2 return 0
      Explore skip: profit=0, holding=false, i=2
        Call7: i=2 return 0
      max(0,0)=0
  max(0,0)=0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Day-by-Day Decision Making with Holding State
ladders.desc: The fundamental unit of deciding action at a single day based on current holding status.
ladders.explanation: This level breaks down the basic decision-making unit: given a particular day with its price, and whether we currently hold a stock, what are the possible actions and their consequences? The intuition is that each day presents a discrete decision point constrained by our current state. If we are holding a stock (bought previously), we have two options: 1) Sell at today's price: this completes a transaction, giving profit = today's price - buy price, and transitions us to 'not holding' state for next day. 2) Skip (hold): do nothing, remain in 'holding' state for next day with same buy price. If we are not holding a stock, we have two options: 1) Buy at today's price: pay today's price to acquire a stock, transition to 'holding' state with buy price = today's price. 2) Skip: do nothing, remain in 'not holding' state. The algorithm for this basic operation is simply enumerating these options based on input state. There's no time/space complexity for this single operation as it's O(1). This connects to L3 as the atomic decision that gets repeated in the recursive traversal - L3 strings together many of these basic operations. Common mistakes: considering invalid options (e.g., buying while holding), miscalculating profit (selling price minus buy price), or not updating state correctly. The pattern is a simple conditional with state-dependent options.

examples.trace:
Current state: holding a stock bought at 8, today's price=10
Option1: Sell
  Action: Sell stock at price 10
  Profit from this transaction: 10 - 8 = 2
  Next state: not holding (no stock)
Option2: Skip/Hold
  Action: Do nothing
  Profit from this decision: 0 (no transaction)
  Next state: still holding (stock still owned, buy price remains 8)
Both options are valid. The choice depends on future prices.

Current state: not holding any stock, today's price=6
Option1: Buy
  Action: Buy stock at price 6
  Cost: pay 6 (negative profit now, potential future profit)
  Next state: holding (stock owned, buy price=6)
Option2: Skip
  Action: Do nothing
  Cost: 0
  Next state: still not holding
Both options are valid. Buying establishes a buy price for potential future selling.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Stock Transaction Profit Calculation
ladders.desc: The basic arithmetic of calculating profit from a single buy-sell transaction.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept: how to calculate profit from a single stock transaction. The intuition is simple financial arithmetic: profit = selling price - buying price. If you buy a stock at price B and later sell it at price S, your profit (or loss if negative) is S - B. This assumes no transaction costs. For example, if you buy at $10 and sell at $15, profit = $15 - $10 = $5. If you buy at $20 and sell at $18, profit = $18 - $20 = -$2 (a loss of $2). The algorithm is trivial: profit = sellPrice - buyPrice. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L4 as the core calculation used when making a sell decision - in L4's 'sell' option, we compute exactly this profit. Common mistakes: subtracting in wrong order (buyPrice - sellPrice), forgetting that profit can be negative, or misunderstanding that buying price is what you paid (not current price). The pattern is simple subtraction with financial interpretation.

examples.trace:
Profit = sellPrice - buyPrice
Profit = 15 - 10
Profit = 5
Interpretation: Bought stock for $10, sold for $15, made $5 profit.

Profit = sellPrice - buyPrice
Profit = 20 - 25
Profit = -5
Interpretation: Bought stock for $25, sold for $20, lost $5.

approaches.name: Peak Valley Greedy O(n)
approaches.summary: Identify all price valleys (local minima) and subsequent peaks (local maxima), summing the differences to capture profit from every upward trend. This mimics buying low and selling high repeatedly based on price patterns.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Peak Valley Greedy Solution for Unlimited Stock Transactions
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the peak-valley greedy algorithm to maximize profit with unlimited buy/sell transactions.
ladders.explanation: The peak-valley greedy approach solves the unlimited transactions stock problem by identifying all profitable upward trends in the price series. The intuition is that we can capture all possible profit by buying at every local minimum (valley) and selling at every subsequent local maximum (peak). This works because any price series can be decomposed into non-overlapping upward segments where buying low and selling high yields maximum cumulative profit. The algorithm scans through prices once, tracking when we're in an upward trend. When prices are rising, we hold the stock; when they start falling, we sell at the previous high point. The key insight is that we don't need to track actual buy/sell points—we can simply accumulate profit whenever today's price is higher than yesterday's, since that represents a profitable one-day transaction that can be part of a larger upward trend. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize total profit to 0. 2) Iterate through prices starting from index 1. 3) If current price > previous price, add the difference to profit. 4) Continue until end of array. Time complexity is O(n) with single pass, space is O(1) using only constant variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete profit accumulation logic. Common mistakes include trying to track actual transaction points (unnecessary), or misunderstanding that we can sum daily positive differences. Real code pattern: def maxProfit(prices): profit = 0; for i in range(1, len(prices)): if prices[i] > prices[i-1]: profit += prices[i] - prices[i-1]; return profit.

examples.trace: Start: profit = 0. i=1: price[1]=1, price[0]=7, 1>7? No, profit=0. i=2: price[2]=5, price[1]=1, 5>1? Yes, profit+=4 → profit=4. i=3: price[3]=3, price[2]=5, 3>5? No, profit=4. i=4: price[4]=6, price[3]=3, 6>3? Yes, profit+=3 → profit=7. i=5: price[5]=4, price[4]=6, 4>6? No, profit=7. End: return 7.
Start: profit = 0. i=1: 2>1? Yes, profit+=1 → profit=1. i=2: 3>2? Yes, profit+=1 → profit=2. i=3: 4>3? Yes, profit+=1 → profit=3. i=4: 5>4? Yes, profit+=1 → profit=4. End: return 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Profit Accumulation from Daily Price Increases
ladders.desc: Core function that accumulates profit by summing positive differences between consecutive days.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the essential profit calculation logic of the peak-valley approach. The intuition is that in an unlimited transactions scenario, we can capture all upward price movements by simply adding up every day-to-day increase. This works because any multi-day upward trend can be decomposed into individual daily increases, and summing these daily profits yields the same total as buying at the start and selling at the end of the trend. The algorithm scans through the price array once, comparing each price with its predecessor. Whenever we find a price increase (current > previous), we add that difference to our running profit total. This elegant simplification avoids the need to explicitly identify valleys and peaks. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize profit to 0. 2) For each index i from 1 to n-1: 3) If prices[i] > prices[i-1], add prices[i] - prices[i-1] to profit. 4) Return profit. Time complexity is O(n) with one pass, space is O(1). This connects to L0 as the complete solution, and to L2 which focuses on the comparison logic. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in array indexing, or incorrectly handling equal prices (no profit when prices are equal). Real code pattern: def accumulateProfit(prices): total = 0; for i in range(1, len(prices)): diff = prices[i] - prices[i-1]; if diff > 0: total += diff; return total.

examples.trace: Start: total = 0. i=1: diff=3-1=2>0, total=2. i=2: diff=2-3=-1≤0, total=2. i=3: diff=4-2=2>0, total=4. End: return 4.
Start: total = 0. i=1: diff=5-5=0≤0, total=0. i=2: diff=5-5=0≤0, total=0. i=3: diff=5-5=0≤0, total=0. End: return 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing Consecutive Elements for Positive Differences
ladders.desc: Identifying when today's price is higher than yesterday's to capture profit opportunities.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental comparison operation that drives the greedy algorithm. The intuition is that profit in stock trading comes from price increases over time. By comparing each day's price with the previous day's price, we can identify immediate profit opportunities. When prices[i] > prices[i-1], this represents a situation where buying yesterday and selling today would yield profit. In the unlimited transactions context, we can take advantage of every such opportunity. The algorithm needs to perform this comparison for every adjacent pair in the array. Step-by-step: 1) Iterate through indices 1 to n-1. 2) For each index i, compute difference = prices[i] - prices[i-1]. 3) Check if difference > 0. This boolean condition determines whether we capture profit from this day pair. Time complexity for just the comparison is O(n), space O(1). This connects to L1 where we use these comparisons to accumulate profit, and to L3 which handles array traversal. Common mistakes include comparing wrong indices (e.g., i with i+1), or misunderstanding that we need strict inequality (≥ would incorrectly count zero differences as profit). Real code pattern: for i in range(1, len(prices)): if prices[i] > prices[i-1]: # Positive difference found.

examples.trace: i=1: prices[1]=5, prices[0]=2, 5>2? Yes → positive difference at index 1. i=2: prices[2]=3, prices[1]=5, 3>5? No. Result: positive differences at indices [1].
i=1: 4>4? No. i=2: 4>4? No. Result: no positive differences.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array with Adjacent Element Access
ladders.desc: Traversing an array while accessing consecutive elements pairs.
ladders.explanation: This level addresses the fundamental skill of iterating through an array while comparing each element with its immediate neighbor. The intuition is that many array algorithms require processing elements in relation to their neighbors. For the stock problem, we need to examine how prices change from one day to the next. This requires a loop that goes through indices where we can access both the current element and the previous one. The key is starting the loop at index 1 (not 0) so prices[i-1] is always valid. Step-by-step: 1) Determine array length n. 2) For i from 1 to n-1 inclusive: 3) Access prices[i] and prices[i-1] as a pair. This gives us consecutive day prices to compare. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal, space O(1). This connects to L2 where we perform comparisons on these pairs, and to L4 which handles basic array access. Common mistakes include starting loop at 0 (causing index error for i-1), going up to n (causing out-of-bounds), or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: n = len(prices); for i in range(1, n): prev = prices[i-1]; curr = prices[i]; # Now compare prev and curr.

examples.trace: Array length=3. i=1: prices[0]=10, prices[1]=20 → pair (10,20). i=2: prices[1]=20, prices[2]=30 → pair (20,30). End: pairs [(10,20), (20,30)].
Array length=1. Loop from i=1 to 0 doesn't execute. No pairs. Result: empty list.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieving values from an array using integer indices.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operation of accessing elements in an array using their positions (indices). The intuition is that arrays store data in contiguous memory locations, and we can retrieve any element if we know its position. For the stock problem, we need to access daily prices by their day number (index). Arrays use zero-based indexing: the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. To access an element, we use the syntax array[index]. It's crucial to ensure the index is within valid bounds (0 to length-1) to avoid errors. Step-by-step: 1) Have an array 'prices' of length n. 2) To get the i-th element (0-indexed), use prices[i]. 3) For example, prices[0] gives first element, prices[n-1] gives last. Time complexity for single access is O(1), space O(1). This connects to L3 where we access multiple elements during iteration, and to L5 which introduces arrays. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common beginner error), accessing out-of-bounds indices, or confusing index with value. Real code pattern: first_price = prices[0]; second_price = prices[1]; last_price = prices[len(prices)-1].

examples.trace: Array has 3 elements. Index 0: prices[0] = 100. Index 2: prices[2] = 300. Result: values [100,300].
Array has 1 element. Index 0: prices[0] = 5. Result: values [5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Sequential Data
ladders.desc: Introduction to arrays as ordered collections of numbers representing daily stock prices.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces arrays, which are ordered collections of elements. In the stock problem context, we use an array to represent daily stock prices over time. The intuition is that we need a data structure to store multiple values in sequence, where the position (index) corresponds to the day number. An array is perfect for this because it maintains order and allows direct access to any day's price. For example, if we have prices [100, 150, 120], this means day 0: $100, day 1: $150, day 2: $120. Arrays have a fixed size (length) determined when created. Key concepts: 1) Elements are stored in contiguous memory. 2) Each element has an index starting from 0. 3) All elements are typically the same type (e.g., all integers). Time complexity for basic operations: access O(1), space O(n) to store n elements. This connects to L4 where we access specific elements. Common beginner mistakes include thinking indices start at 1, confusing array length with last index, or not understanding that arrays preserve order. Real code pattern: prices = [100, 150, 120] # Creates array; len(prices) gives 3.

examples.input: Create array for prices: day1=50, day2=60, day3=55
examples.trace: Day numbers map to indices: day1 → index 0 = 50, day2 → index 1 = 60, day3 → index 2 = 55. Result: array [50,60,55] with length 3.
Only one day: day1 → index 0 = 100. Result: array [100] with length 1.

testCases.input: Create array for prices: day1=50, day2=60, day3=55

approaches.name: One-Pass Greedy O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through prices once, adding the profit whenever the next day's price is higher than the current day's, effectively capturing all positive daily differences. This is optimal as it accumulates profit from every price increase.

ladders.title: Full Problem — One-Pass Greedy Solution for Maximum Profit with Unlimited Transactions
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the greedy algorithm that iterates through prices once, accumulating profit from every price increase.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition behind this approach is that in a sequence of daily stock prices, the maximum profit with unlimited transactions can be achieved by capturing every local price increase. Since we can buy and sell unlimited times, we don't need to hold stocks for long periods - we can simply buy whenever we expect the price to increase the next day and sell immediately. This is mathematically equivalent to summing all positive differences between consecutive days. The algorithm works because any optimal solution can be decomposed into these daily transactions without reducing total profit. We iterate through the price array from index 1 to n-1, comparing each price with the previous day's price. If today's price is higher than yesterday's, we add the difference to our total profit. This captures all upward movements in the price graph. The time complexity is O(n) since we make a single pass through the array, and space complexity is O(1) as we only maintain a running total. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete profit accumulation logic. Common mistakes include overcomplicating the solution with unnecessary state tracking or trying to find 'best' buy-sell pairs instead of simply accumulating daily gains. Another mistake is starting the loop at index 0 instead of 1, which would cause index errors when comparing with previous day. The real code pattern involves a simple for loop with a conditional addition.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize total_profit = 0
2. For i from 1 to n-1 (where n = length of prices array):
   a. If prices[i] > prices[i-1]:
      total_profit += prices[i] - prices[i-1]
3. Return total_profit

Time: O(n) - single pass through array
Space: O(1) - constant extra space

examples.trace:
Initialize profit = 0
Day 1: price=1, prev=7, diff=-6 (negative, skip)
Day 2: price=5, prev=1, diff=4, profit=0+4=4
Day 3: price=3, prev=5, diff=-2 (negative, skip)
Day 4: price=6, prev=3, diff=3, profit=4+3=7
Day 5: price=4, prev=6, diff=-2 (negative, skip)
Final profit = 7
Explanation: Buy at 1, sell at 5 (+4). Buy at 3, sell at 6 (+3). Total = 7

Initialize profit = 0
Day 1: price=2, prev=1, diff=1, profit=0+1=1
Day 2: price=3, prev=2, diff=1, profit=1+1=2
Day 3: price=4, prev=3, diff=1, profit=2+1=3
Day 4: price=5, prev=4, diff=1, profit=3+1=4
Final profit = 4
Explanation: Multiple strategies work. One approach: buy at 1, sell at 5 (+4). Greedy approach: buy/sell each day: (1→2)+(2→3)+(3→4)+(4→5) = 1+1+1+1 = 4

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Accumulating Positive Daily Differences
ladders.desc: The core profit accumulation logic that sums positive differences between consecutive elements.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine implements the essential insight of the greedy approach: maximum profit equals the sum of all positive daily price changes. The intuition is that in a price sequence, any upward movement represents a profit opportunity. Since we can transact unlimited times, we can capture each upward movement independently. Mathematically, if we have prices p[0], p[1], ..., p[n-1], the maximum profit equals Σ max(0, p[i] - p[i-1]) for i=1 to n-1. This works because any optimal trading strategy can be broken down into these daily transactions without loss. The algorithm scans through the array, comparing each price with its predecessor. When the current price is higher, it adds the difference to a running total. This is essentially finding all local minima followed immediately by local maxima. The time complexity is O(n) for the single pass, and space is O(1). This connects to L0 as the complete solution simply wraps this logic in a function. It connects to L2 as this implements the 'capture every increase' technique. Common mistakes include incorrect loop boundaries (should start at i=1), forgetting to handle empty or single-element arrays, or trying to optimize further when the simple approach is already optimal. Another error is using absolute values instead of only positive differences. The real code pattern is a for loop with conditional accumulation.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize accumulator = 0
2. For i from 1 to length(prices)-1:
   a. current_diff = prices[i] - prices[i-1]
   b. If current_diff > 0: accumulator += current_diff
3. Return accumulator

Time: O(n) - linear scan
Space: O(1) - constant space

examples.trace:
Initialize acc = 0
Compare 2-5 = -3 (≤0, skip)
Compare 8-2 = 6 (>0), acc=0+6=6
Compare 4-8 = -4 (≤0, skip)
Compare 9-4 = 5 (>0), acc=6+5=11
Wait, correction: 9-4=5, so acc=6+5=11
Actually recheck: prices=[5,2,8,4,9]
i=1: 2-5=-3 skip
i=2: 8-2=6, acc=6
i=3: 4-8=-4 skip
i=4: 9-4=5, acc=6+5=11
Output should be 11, not 10. My mistake in initial calculation.
Final accumulator = 11

Initialize acc = 0
Compare 9-10 = -1 (≤0, skip)
Compare 8-9 = -1 (≤0, skip)
Compare 7-8 = -1 (≤0, skip)
Compare 6-7 = -1 (≤0, skip)
Final accumulator = 0
All differences are negative or zero, so no profit opportunities.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Capturing Every Price Increase
ladders.desc: The fundamental technique of identifying and summing all upward price movements between consecutive days.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic represents the key insight of the greedy approach: in a sequence where unlimited transactions are allowed, the optimal strategy is to profit from every price increase. The intuition is that if the price increases from day i-1 to day i, we could have bought on day i-1 and sold on day i for a profit of prices[i] - prices[i-1]. Since transactions have no limits, we can do this for every such increase. This is equivalent to decomposing the price graph into its upward segments. Mathematically, for any optimal solution, we can show that it cannot outperform simply taking all positive daily differences. If we skip a positive difference, we're missing a profit opportunity. If we try to combine multiple days, we're not gaining anything beyond the sum of individual increases. The algorithm implementation involves scanning and comparing adjacent elements. This connects to L1 as that's where this logic gets implemented as code. It connects to L3 as this builds on the concept of analyzing consecutive pairs. Common mistakes include thinking we need to find 'best' pairs or worrying about transaction costs. Another error is misunderstanding why this works - it's not about predicting the future, but about reacting to observed increases. Some learners try to find local minima and maxima separately, which is unnecessary. The real code pattern is simple element comparison in a loop.

Step-by-step conceptual process:
1. Look at each adjacent pair (day i-1 and day i)
2. If price increases: that represents a profit opportunity
3. Sum all such increases

Time: O(n) conceptually
Space: O(1) conceptually

examples.trace:
Analyze consecutive pairs:
Pair (3,8): 8>3, increase of 5
Pair (8,2): 2<8, decrease (skip)
Pair (2,10): 10>2, increase of 8
Total increases: 5 + 8 = 13
This means: Buy at 3, sell at 8 (+5). Buy at 2, sell at 10 (+8). Total=13

Analyze consecutive pairs:
Pair (5,5): 5=5, no increase
Pair (5,5): 5=5, no increase
Pair (5,5): 5=5, no increase
Total increases: 0
No price increases, so no profit opportunities.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Consecutive Array Elements
ladders.desc: The skill of iterating through an array while comparing each element with its immediate neighbor.
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on the fundamental array traversal pattern where we examine pairs of consecutive elements. The intuition is that many array algorithms require comparing each element with its neighbors to identify patterns, trends, or changes. In the stock problem context, we need to detect when prices increase from one day to the next. The technique involves starting from the second element (index 1) and comparing it with the previous element (index 0), then moving through the array. This is more efficient than nested loops and captures local relationships. The key insight is that we only need to look at immediate neighbors to identify daily price changes. This connects to L2 as that's where we use these comparisons to find price increases. It connects to L4 as this builds on basic array indexing. Common mistakes include starting comparisons from index 0 (which has no previous element), going out of bounds at the end, or using the wrong comparison direction. Another error is trying to compare non-adjacent elements when only adjacent comparisons are needed. The real code pattern involves a for loop from 1 to n-1 with prices[i] and prices[i-1] comparisons.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. For i from 1 to length(arr)-1:
   a. current = arr[i]
   b. previous = arr[i-1]
   c. Compare current and previous as needed

Time: O(n) for linear traversal
Space: O(1) for storing current and previous

examples.trace:
Array: [4,9,3,7]
i=1: current=9, previous=4, compare: 9>4 = true
i=2: current=3, previous=9, compare: 3>9 = false
i=3: current=7, previous=3, compare: 7>3 = true
We've compared all consecutive pairs: (4,9), (9,3), (3,7)

Array: [1,1,1,1]
i=1: current=1, previous=1, compare: 1>1 = false
i=2: current=1, previous=1, compare: 1>1 = false
i=3: current=1, previous=1, compare: 1>1 = false
All comparisons show no increase (equal values)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Traversal with Index Arithmetic
ladders.desc: The fundamental skill of looping through an array while correctly accessing elements using indices.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation covers the essential programming pattern of iterating through an array while performing index arithmetic to access elements. The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, and we often need to process elements in sequence. The key concepts are: 1) Arrays are zero-indexed (first element at index 0), 2) We can use loop variables as indices, 3) We can calculate related indices (like i-1 for previous element). For the stock problem, we need to access both the current element and the previous one during iteration. This involves starting the loop at index 1 to ensure index i-1 exists. The algorithm is straightforward: initialize a loop counter, check termination condition, access array elements using the counter, and increment the counter. This connects to L3 as that's where we use this traversal to compare consecutive elements. It connects to L5 as this builds on understanding array indexing. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (accessing arr[n] when valid indices are 0 to n-1), incorrect loop boundaries, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Another error is modifying the loop variable incorrectly inside the loop. The real code pattern is a standard for loop with index variable.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Let n = length of array
2. For i from 1 to n-1:
   a. Access arr[i] and arr[i-1]
   b. Perform desired operation

Time: O(n) for visiting each relevant index once
Space: O(1) for index variables

examples.input: Array=[10,20,30], access pattern: arr[i] and arr[i-1] for i=1,2
examples.trace:
Array indices: 0:10, 1:20, 2:30
Loop i from 1 to 2:
i=1: arr[1] = 20, arr[0] = 10
i=2: arr[2] = 30, arr[1] = 20
We successfully accessed each element pair: (10,20) and (20,30)

examples.input: Array=[5], access pattern: arr[i] and arr[i-1] for i=1
examples.trace:
Array has length 1, so valid indices are 0 only.
Loop would be from i=1 to 0 (since n-1=0), which is an empty range.
Therefore no iterations occur - correctly handles edge case.

testCases.input: Array=[10,20,30], access pattern: arr[i] and arr[i-1] for i=1,2
Array=[5], access pattern: arr[i] and arr[i-1] for i=1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: The most basic prerequisite: how arrays store elements and how to access them using indices.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation covers the absolute basics of array data structures. An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type. Each element has an index (position) starting from 0. The intuition is that arrays provide random access - we can directly access any element if we know its index. For example, in array [10, 20, 30], index 0 contains 10, index 1 contains 20, and index 2 contains 30. The key concepts are: 1) Zero-based indexing (first element at index 0), 2) Array length (number of elements), 3) Valid indices range from 0 to length-1. Accessing an element uses the syntax arr[index]. This is fundamental because in the stock problem, we need to access daily prices stored in an array. This connects to L4 as that's where we use indexing in loops. Common mistakes include thinking arrays are 1-indexed, accessing indices outside the valid range (causing errors), or confusing the index with the element value. Beginners often write arr[1] thinking it means 'first element' rather than 'element at position 1' (which is actually the second element). Another common error is forgetting that arrays have fixed size. The real code pattern is simply using square brackets with an index.

Step-by-step explanation:
1. Declare an array: prices = [7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4]
2. Length = 6 (indices 0-5)
3. prices[0] = 7
4. prices[1] = 1
5. prices[2] = 5
... up to prices[5] = 4

Time: O(1) for single access
Space: O(n) for storing the array

examples.trace:
Array has 3 elements at positions 0,1,2
Access arr[0]: returns first element = 5
Access arr[1]: returns second element = 10
Access arr[2]: returns third element = 15
All accesses are valid (indices 0-2 within range 0 to 2)

Array has 1 element at position 0
Access arr[0]: returns the only element = 100
Index 0 is valid (within range 0 to 0)
